RULING: A player may play a provisional ball under Rule 18.3 if they reasonably believe their original ball: 
1) May be lost outside a penalty area, or
2) May be out of bounds.

The player must announce their intention to play a provisional ball before taking any other action. This allows the player to keep play moving while searching for the original ball. If the original ball is found within 3 minutes and is in play, the player must continue with that ball. If the original ball is lost or out of bounds, the player must then proceed under the applicable stroke-and-distance penalty (Rule 18.2).

The option to play a provisional ball is provided under Rule 18.3 of the Rules of Golf. This allows the player to save time if the original ball is not found or is out of bounds, without having to return to the previous spot to play a ball under penalty of stroke and distance.

RULING: The overflow area outside the marked boundaries of the pond is considered casual water.

Under Rule 16.1a(1), casual water is "any temporary accumulation of water on the course that is not in a water hazard and is visible before or after the player takes a stance." Since the pond has overflowed its marked boundaries, the area of water outside those boundaries is considered casual water, not part of the water hazard.

The player would be entitled to free relief from the casual water under Rule 16.1b. This allows the player to take relief, without penalty, by dropping a ball in a relief area that is either one club-length from the nearest point of complete relief, or on the opposite side of the casual water equidistant from the hole.

response:
RULING: The player is the sole judge of whether their ball is unplayable.

Under Rule 19.1 of the Rules of Golf, the player is the one who determines if their ball is unplayable. The player may declare their ball unplayable at any place on the course except when the ball is in a penalty area. The decision to declare the ball unplayable is at the player's sole discretion.

If the player decides their ball is unplayable, they have three options for relief under Rule 19.2:
1. Take stroke-and-distance relief by playing the ball from where the previous stroke was made (Rule 19.2a).
2. Drop the ball in a relief area within two club-lengths of the spot where the ball lies, not nearer the hole (Rule 19.2b).
3. Drop the ball on a straight line from the hole through the spot where the ball lies, going back as far as desired (Rule 19.2c).

The player must select one of these three options and proceed accordingly, taking a one-stroke penalty.

According to Rule 19.1 in the Rules of Golf, a player is entitled to take unplayable ball relief at any time, provided they have not yet made a stroke at the next stroke from the spot where the original ball was last played. 

Since you have not put another ball into play, you can still choose to take free relief under the unplayable ball rule. This would involve taking relief within one of the three options allowed under Rule 19.1:

2) Take a one-stroke penalty and drop a ball on a straight line from the hole through the spot where the ball lay.
3) Take a one-stroke penalty and go back in the direction from the hole as far as you wish and drop a ball.

You can change your decision and properly take unplayable ball relief as long as you have not yet played your next stroke. This flexibility allows you to reconsider the situation and select the best relief option.

Your opponent's ball was overhanging the hole, meaning it was within the leather of the flagstick and therefore considered as holed once the reasonable time to reach the hole (40 seconds) plus an additional 10 seconds had elapsed. RULING: No, you cannot drop in the good sand area within the bunker. When the entire bunker is marked as ground under repair (GUR), you must take full relief outside the bunker under Rule 16.1c.

Rule 16.1c states that if your ball is in a abnormal course condition (such as GUR), you may take free relief by dropping the ball in and playing it from the relief area. However, this relief must be taken outside the bunker, not within the same bunker even if there is a good sand area nearby. The entire bunker is considered the abnormal course condition, so you cannot obtain relief within it.

Your options are:
1) Play the ball as it lies in the GUR bunker
2) Take free relief by dropping the ball outside the bunker, within one club-length of the nearest point of complete relief, no nearer the hole.

RULING: You are not required to play the ball dropped for lateral relief. As long as you have not made a stroke at the dropped ball, you may still choose to take stroke-and-distance relief back to the tee.

Rule 19.2a allows you to take unplayable ball relief, including the option to play again from the original spot under stroke-and-distance. The fact that your caddie has already dropped a ball does not commit you to that relief option. You may still choose to go back to the tee under Rule 19.2a(i) instead.

However, once you make a stroke at the dropped ball, you would be required to continue playing it under the lateral relief procedure.
